verdict_reasoning: LYN is a Src-family non-receptor tyrosine kinase. It is a cytoplasmic protein with N-terminal lipidation (myristoylation and palmitoylation) that enables stable association with the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane and other endomembranes (endosomes, lysosomes). Under baseline conditions, LYN resides predominantly in the cytoplasm and on the cytoplasmic face of the PM and intracellular compartments. However, LYN is an upstream signaling hub in B-cell and mast-cell activation, and upon cell activation (e.g., BCR or FcɛRI engagement) it rapidly relocates from the cytoplasm to the PM and associates transiently with activated receptor complexes on the outer leaflet. Additionally, in certain disease contexts (particularly hematologic malignancies and some immune activations), LYN undergoes aberrant surface relocalization or is recruited to surface immune receptors. Cell-surface-directed therapeutics (especially in lymphoid malignancies) have targeted LYN indirectly via downstream signaling. However, the core topology is inner-leaflet lipidated; surface accessibility under baseline conditions is minimal. The transient surface recruitment upon activation and documented disease-state relocalization place it in the contextual bucket rather than a clear 'no'.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Whether activation-induced transient PM surface association (at the outer leaflet face of activated receptor signaling hubs) meets the threshold for 'contextual' or whether the predominantly cytoplasmic / inner-leaflet baseline should demote this to 'no'. Direct binder accessibility from extracellular face under baseline is very limited; under activation, LYN is at the PM but mostly still inner-leaflet-proximal.
